context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda lost on this trade/outsourcing/labor standards reflection tbh. idk how to connect trade openness, growth, and labor rights all in one piece.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point to acknowledge the complexity! Let's break the ice. What's your gut feeling: do you think countries that trade more openly tend to grow faster economically? Can you think of any real-world examples that support or challenge that idea?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion about how to connect the themes. Rather than breaking down the assignment for them, I should use a Socratic question to help them identify what they already know and where to start. I'll ask a targeted opening question that gets them thinking about the core empirical claim in the prompt first - the trade-growth correlation - since that's the foundation the rest of the piece builds on.

exchanges.student: umm my gut says yes but then China/India make me second guess it lol. like they grew a lot, but idk if that was from being “open” or from the state doing a ton.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're sensing is actually the heart of the question. So what does 'open to trade' even mean in the case of China or India? Were they fully open, or did they open selectively? That distinction might help you resolve your second-guessing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually landed on a really important tension in the prompt without realizing it. China and India are specifically mentioned in the assignment, and the student is already intuiting the nuance that state intervention complicates the trade-openness-growth relationship. I should affirm the direction of their thinking and push them to go deeper on what 'openness' actually means in those cases, rather than giving them more scaffolding.

exchanges.student: yeah that’s where I’m stuck rn, bc “selectively open” sounds right but also kinda vague. idk what counts as selective vs actually open.
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: what are some tools a government could use to control *how much* foreign competition enters its market? Even everyday examples work here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on a definitional question. Rather than defining 'selective openness' for them, I should push them to think about concrete policy tools that governments use to manage trade, which will help them arrive at the distinction themselves. This keeps the Socratic method going and moves toward the solution.

exchanges.student: uh tariffs maybe, quotas, like rules on imports? but then I lose the bigger point and start spiraling tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the tools. Now zoom out - if China and India used those tools strategically while still growing fast, what does that tell you about the direct correlation between openness and growth that the prompt is asking about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified trade policy tools but is self-aware about losing the bigger picture. I should validate their answer briefly and redirect them to the bigger argument they need to make in their reflection piece, rather than going deeper into trade policy mechanics which could be a detour.

exchanges.student: idk, can u just explain the whole trade-growth part in a simple way? I feel like I’m missing the main idea fr.
exchanges.tutor: I won't do that - but here's the thing: you've actually already gotten there yourself. You said China and India grew a lot but weren't fully open. So in your own words, does that support or contradict the idea that openness *directly* causes growth?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the concept directly, which would violate the core rule of never giving the answer. I should refuse politely but redirect them back to their own reasoning, which has actually been quite good. They've already identified the key tension themselves - I should point that out and push them to articulate it.

exchanges.student: i guess it contradicts “directly” bc growth can happen with partial opening too. but then idk how to say the historical record part without sounding fake.
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning. Now, beyond China and India, can you think of any other historical examples - countries or periods - where the trade-growth relationship was more complicated than 'open = grow'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the historical record contradicts a simple direct correlation. Now they're worried about how to express this authentically in their writing. I should not help them with language or writing - that's explicitly prohibited. Instead, I should push them to think about what historical evidence they can draw on to support the claim they just made, which will naturally give their writing substance.

exchanges.student: honestly no, that’s why I’m stressed lol. i keep blanking and then the labor rights part makes it worse too.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built a solid argument already - selective openness can drive growth, and the direct correlation is questionable. That's a real intellectual position. Rather than hunting for more examples right now, let's move forward: why do you think labor rights specifically come up in a discussion about trade openness?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and feels stuck. They've been circling the trade-growth relationship for several exchanges now and are starting to feel overwhelmed. Rather than dwelling further on historical examples, I should acknowledge their progress, remind them of the bigger picture of the assignment, and gently push them toward connecting the trade-growth argument they've built to the labor rights dimension, which is equally important to the reflection.

exchanges.student: idk maybe bc countries compete by keeping wages low or standards weak? but I’m not sure if that’s too simplistic.
exchanges.tutor: That's not simplistic at all - that's a real dynamic scholars debate. So if countries are competing by weakening labor standards, who do you think should be responsible for keeping them in check?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently arrived at the 'race to the bottom' concept, which is a key idea in the assignment's context. I should affirm this is not simplistic at all - it's a legitimate concept - and push them to think about who bears responsibility for monitoring this dynamic, which is the last part of the prompt they haven't touched yet.

exchanges.student: maybe governments, but also like the ILO or trade groups? ngl I’m still fuzzy on who actually monitors this stuff.
exchanges.tutor: You've now touched on every major part of the prompt - trade-growth correlation, China/India as cases, labor standards competition, and monitoring actors. That's enough to write with. What would your opening argument be if you started drafting right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified key actors (governments, ILO, trade groups) and is being honest about their uncertainty. We're getting close to the end of the planned conversation length and the student has now touched on all major parts of the prompt: trade-growth correlation, historical record, China/India, labor standards race to the bottom, and monitoring. I should push them to start integrating all of this into a written attempt rather than continuing to explore concepts in the abstract.

exchanges.student: tbh I still can’t see how it fits together in one argument. can u give me a super short thesis idea maybe?
exchanges.tutor: I can't do that - writing is entirely yours to do. But look back at what you've already told me: trade openness doesn't directly cause growth, selective opening can work, labor standards get weakened in competition, and monitoring is fragmented. Can you try to connect those ideas in one sentence yourself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write or suggest language for their thesis, which is directly prohibited by the rules. I must refuse this clearly. However, I can help them synthesize by asking them to connect the dots themselves using what they've already said in our conversation.
